titleStmt.title: Napoli, Biblioteca Nazionale, MS XIII.B.29. Edizione diplomatica e interpretativa

msItem.p: Altri testi in prosa e versi

objectDesc.p: Codice pergamenaceo, 146 fogli. Dettagli bibliografici in Guddatt-Figge, G., Catalogue of Manuscripts containing Middle English Romances, Munich, 1976.
Una sola mano, stile secretariale. Mani successive visibili nelle note e nei disegni.

editorialDecl.p: Facsimile ed edizione interpretativa, dove si è provveduto alla normalizzazione grafematica, alla correzione degli errori scribali e all'aggiunta della punteggiatura secondo gli standard moderni.

div.p: Prosa Ricette brevi per la cura di vari disturbi

p.hi: Il ricettario non è attestato in altri testimoni

div.p:
o helpe a woman in travel of childe
Take a da kdark tone and have it to powdir and ye veyeve it to to traveling woman to
drinke wyne and she shalbe deliuerid anon . a Alo take the iue of leke and yeve
hir to drinke or els yeve hir to drinke detany

ffFor the dieae aftir hir travaile
Take an oynon and sethe it in watir Ssoft and malwe rote and yolkis of eggis
harde soden and grinde al thee to gadir with oyle of olif and make a platir
ley and ley it hote ther totherto as þe wymmen wol fynde

o deliuer a woman of childe dede or quike
Write and put to hur wombe aria peit remedi anna celi abeccam Cecilia
remedi ator arepo tenet oa rotas Also yeve hur betany or ope
her owne watir and sethe ham in wyne and yeue hur to drenke

oo so hathe the poe
Take rewe comyn ethe hem bothe in a panne hony and pepir ete a
sponeful at morowe a nothiranothir at none and anothir at nyȝt

Anothir medecyne for the ame
Take rede mynte rewe of ei like moche stamp hem wring out the ue and
put therof in his notrel whan he gothe to bed it hal a mendeamende him

To retore mannys complexion
Take fenelede skirwit of eche ijtwo un medle it newe Ccowe mylke make
pelettis therof and yeve the sike iijthree at morows and iijthree at evyn make pelett
as grete as abenea bene bul ete him be a noyntidanoyntid bi forebifore þe Iueiuse of oyle morelle
and venegre temprid to gadirtogadir a boutabout hithis membris

A notherAnother for the same
Take an olde cok choppid and toppid in a pot bi hit silue with outwithout lico put to
gingir galingale notilede ugir al made in poudre þan boile it in a nothiranothir
potte watir and lete it sethe ijtwo daies and ijtwo niȝtis / and etyn retorith
man ismannis complexion lost þrowe sekenys or els bi wommen also who lutith
moche to wymmen vseuseoft cyron hote swete mylke

p.l: Take the wat of efroos wahe ham and thei hul be hole out dowt

div.l: Take poudre of arafetida and medle it boris grece and put it in a erthin pot that hath
an hole in the side and put in tointo the hole a fewe quik colis and go to place therwher the
Comyescomyes be and put the poudre in the colis and tond inthein the wynde syde and a nonanon as thei
fele sauour therof thei wol come aboute thi fete for craft thou mait lede ham
whedir lut and when thou lut to voide ham lete out the fire with thi fote
Take an hondful of tany and tamp it male and tem it with white wyne and then
trayne it throw a cloth and whan it is treyned put ther totherto a poneful of treacle in
to the ue and lete the sike go to his nakid bed nd when he is leide in his bed yeue
him the drinke and lete him sit p in his bed the he cat not and ley clothe ynow
apone him and yef he swete he is sauf
Take betanye morus diaboli myllefoile fethir voy and boile thee with watir and
aftir with sugre and e this drink
Take harde paynisch sope and put in a little tale ale in a up a yenstayent cuppis
bottome til the ale be al white and then shave a peny wiȝt of good yvory lete the ike
drink firt and lat and it shal amende him
Take the watir of elendyne and watir of arowe ther ther and drink it and hit helpith
sone
Take yvorie and safron of eche liche moche and put it in good ale or in wyne and
drinke it IXnine daies and thowe halte be hole
Take a pomegarnet and tamp shelle and alle and tem it good ale or good
wyne and then yeue the sike to drinke therof and thou halt be hole
Take iijthree levis of peletorie os payne and iijthree levis of sange and iijthree levis of safron as
it growth and as many chindis as bith in the flowris and take as many
pepir cornys as bith chindis in the iijthree flowris and stampe al thee to gadirtogadir
and lete the sike drinke therof draf and alle to gadirtogadir y tempridytemprid ale
Take warmot and sethe it longe in watir and wache the sike that watir iijthree tymes
right welle and yeue him to drinke euery day that erbe schrad in wyne and halbe
hot bote

l.fw: fforFor al maner of tothe ache

div.l: Take the rote of sporge and sethe it in wyne and with thi wyne wahe þi monthmouth
onys in a moneth and it hal helpe the of the tothe ache
Take the marowe of an hors and ther withtherwith a noynteanoynte the sore tothe and it halbe hole
lo take the rote of oemarie and chewe it and it voidiþ ache a nonanon
lo take the rote of peletorie of Englond of f Spayne and lei it on ide that
the tothe akith and it helpith sone and the rote of henbane dothe the ame

l.fw: A medecyne uid for the tothe che

div.l: Take the us of fethir voi and do it on the ere on the hole ide and top it with a
litil coton and lete the pacient ret him on the ore side and the ache hal voide
sone

l.fw: Anoynement for al maner of ache

div.l: Take sange þewe warmot locenlevis and tamp ham wel to gadirtogadir and melle
ther withtherwith may botir and lete it roti Xtendaies and aftir fry hit frankencene
and it is good for the ache

l.fw: A drinke to ditroy the hed ache

div.l: Take betanye and vervayne warmot and elidon and vfive cornys of pe
hony and sethe ham al in watir and kepe the watir in a viol of glas and
drinke therof fating and e it and it hal saue the from al evil in hed
Take an hondful of betanye and a nothiranothir of varvayne hondful amanyll
hondful warmot and ham in rennyng watir and wahe the sike issikes hed
and take the same erbis whan thei be soden and wring ham and grinde ham
in a morter and tem it the same licour a yenayen and put ther tothertowhete bran
for to holde in the lico and make a chapelet of the ame a kerchef wynde
in the sike issikes hed and ley the platire with inwithin the chapelet as hote as he may suffre
Ve this iijthree nyȝtis and the ike halbe hole on warantie as eieth pocrae good leche
Take camamyl boile it wyne ley it to the vaynes and to templis for it is good
Take peletory of ayne and chewe the rotis iijthree daies a good quantite it chal purge
the hed and put a weyaweythe ache and fateny the tethe inthein the gommys
Take iiijfour hondful of barly corne and bray it male ther iij three hondfull
of betayne and melle theithem with a litil white wyne and fyue worte double the wy
and lete it be as thik as pap and boile hit til the wyne and the wort be al motealmoste
watid and than ley it platir wie to the side of the hed that is grevid as hote as
it may be suffrid and do this oft
Take white cooe and strong ale and boile ham to gadirtogadir vn tounto halfendele and
dip a lynnen cloth ther intherin as hote as it may be suffrid ley it to the sike issikes forehed

l.fw: fforFor the megryn webbe or perle

div.l: Take ijtwo hondful of efferos and stamp it smale in a mort and than frye it
in a frieng pan capons grece barow isbarowis grece and gose grece ther oftherof make
here of salue and se this a litil in your ye and thei schul be hole
Take a hondful of elidony a nothiranothir of betayne a litil of camomyl
sethe ham in an erthyn pot that licour come neuir yn and cat ther totherto a
of comyn and lete it sethe wel al to gadirtogadir and as hote atas thou mai suffre
ompe thi hedde ouer the pot and couer wel thi hed that ho brethe go/outgo out and
se this medecyne iijthree daies at morowe and at eve and the ike chal be hole
with inwithin iijthree daies
Take a grete hondul of ungilauris and clarifie ham clene and bray ham in
a morter and take the powdir of xxtwenty baies and iijthree peny wiȝt of powdir of ewe
nd tem this to gadirtogadir and make ther oftherof a plater and ley it to as it garni the

l.fw: To do a weyawey a web in a mannys e

div.l: Take the grece of an ele and tem it woman iswomans mylke and drop/itdrop it in tointo the ore e
and þe web hal a weyawey
Take peritorie samite omynyle and pis tampid and oden to gadirtogadir
and this is a good laxatif

div.p:
Trama (in inglese)
Sir Isumbras is a rich and courteous knight, with a beautiful wife and three sons, who has forgotten that he owes his good fortune to God. One day, a bird sent by Christ reminds him of his sin and offers him a choice between suffering in youth or old age. Isumbras repents, and promises to serve Christ while he is young and strong. He calmly accepts his fate as his horse dies, his hawks and hounds flee and his property burns down. He and his family leave the county and wander where Christ leads them, begging for food. As they are crossing a river, Isumbras’ two eldest sons are carried away by a lion and a leopard. Isumbras urges his wife and youngest son to accept God’s will, and the three continue to the Greek sea, where they find the war ships of a Saracen king. Isumbras refuses to convert in return for food and rejects the king’s offer to purchase his wife. She is forcibly taken from him, and he is left, badly beaten, with a cloth full of gold. Before the ships sail the pair is granted a final meeting, where she gives him half of her ring and tells him to search for her. Distraught, Isumbras continues on his way, until a griffin steals his gold and his youngest son is taken by a unicorn. Left alone, he submits himself to God’s will. He joins a community of ironsmiths and spends seven years carrying ore and learning to make armour. When he hears that the Saracens are waging war against a Christian ruler, Isumbras rides a work horse into battle and defeats them, killing their king. The Christian king thanks the mysterious knight and takes him to a nunnery. As soon as his wounds are healed, however, Isumbras dresses as a pilgrim, and continues to wander. He sails to the Holy Land and spends seven years living in poverty until another angel informs him that his sins are forgiven. He then travels to the court of a charitable queen, but weeps instead of eating the feast that is laid before him. Full of pity, the queen gives him a room in her castle, where he regains his strength and beats her best knights in a tournament. One day Isumbras finds a nest containing his cloth and gold, which he conceals under his bed. When the jealous Saracens discover it, they show it to the queen, who realises that it belonged to her husband. Isumbras tells his story and shows her his ring. She produces her half, and the couple are reunited. Isumbras is crowned and decrees the kingdom Christian, but his men desert him when he is attacked by the Saracens. As he and his wife prepare to fight, they are joined by three knights, riding on a leopard, a lion and a unicorn. The five of them defeat the Saracen host, and the knights reveal that they are Isumbras’ sons, sent back to him by God. The family rejoices, and conquers the lands of another three Saracen kings, crowning Isumbras’ sons and converting their inhabitants. When they die, their souls all go to heaven.

Altri testimoni
Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 175, fols. 98r–107r
Edinburgh, National Library of Scotland, MS Advocates 19.3.1, fols. 48r–56v
Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91 ('Thornton Manuscript'), fols. 109r–114v
London, British Library, MS Cotton Caligula A.ii, fols. 130r–134r
London, Gray's Inn, MS 20, fols. 228r228-v
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 61, fols. 9r–16r
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Douce 261, fols. 1r–7v
Oxford, University College, MS 142, fol. 128r

div.l: e made be hevyn erthe
al worlde in daies seuyn
that is ful of myȝt
send s alle his bleyng
las and more olde and yong
and kepe s day and nyȝt
wol you telle of a knyȝt
that was douȝty in ilke a fiȝt
in towne and eke in fielde
ther durt no man his dynt a bideabide
with pere ne with childe
an he was cuthe y noweynowe
x to drawe in his plowe
and tedis in his talle
man he was curteye and kynde
euery man was his frende
he was lord of alle
Curteis and kynde he was
his name was clepid ir Iombrase
othe curteis and fre
the gretti of his curtey
ne couthe ther no man ditry
fulle good man was he
Eke men he louid in halle
and ȝaue ham obus of riche palle
bothe golde and eke fe
f curteie he was kyng
his curtefie had no endyng
n worlde is none so fre
s feire a wife had he
as eny in cuthe miȝt be
as telle it ȝowe
and knowe childryn had he thre
as feire as eny myȝt be
for they were feire y noweynowe
Into his hert a pride was brouȝt
of god isGodis worke he had no rouȝt
his merci for to nomme
So longe he vayned in hiȝe de
that od uolde no lengue a bideabide
but end to him a tewyne
So hit bi fillebifille ppon a day
the knyȝt went furthe him to play
his forest for to e
and as he went in a narowe way
a fowle he hird him to say
that sate ppon a tre
and aid elcome Iumbras
hou hat for ȝeteforȝete whate thou was
for pride and golde an fe
he kynge of heuyn wel gretith the
in ȝouthe o elde thou getit pouerte
nd chee whethir thou haddist le tobe to be
ith careful hert and sikyng seresore
he fille ppon his kneys there
and his hondis p he hilde
orldis welthe wol for sakeforsake
and to ristis merci me take
myne hert to hym ȝilde
n yowthe may ride and go
in lde may nouȝt do so
my bonis wol wax olde
ord ȝif thi wille be
yn youthe send hit me
and welthe in myne elde
wey the fowle takith his fliȝt
and al a lonealone leuys the knyȝt
ful sone he went his way
nd whan he had the fowle for loreforlore
his stede that was so liȝt bi forebifore
undir him gan die
is houndis and his hawkis bothe
wenten to wode to him were lothe
ilke one to dyuers way
hat wondir was þan þei he were wo
n fote him heuith for to go
to payne turnyth alle his play
nd also by the wode he went
a litil knaue was to him sent
that come hym a ȝayneaȝayne
he come to tal him a won tithing
that brende walwas al his thing
and his bestis slayne
ord ther nys nouȝt left on lyue
but ȝowre childryn and ȝoure wyue
i that me bouȝt
he seid ȝif thei may on lyue be
mi wife and my childryn iijthree
alle drede me riȝt nouȝt
nd alle he went silue alone
his hurde men he met ilke one
he seidhate eylith yowe
ey seid fee was from hem revid
ord no thingnothing is yowe bi leuydbileuyd
an ox to youre plowe
hey wept alle and did hem ille
the knyȝt bade ham alle be stille
ne wite yow this wo
od ȝewith s and od take
and at is wille riche e make
and pore men also
deleful siȝt he gan ther se
his wife and his children thre
out of the fyre were fled
as nakid as they as thei were borne
they sittith stille ndur a thorne
brent out of hare bed
e chaungid he no thingnothing of his ble
tille he sawe ham nakid be
that ere were comely clad
he lady bade ham be blithe
your fadre come ful swithe
for no thinge be ye a dradadrad
hey wepte alle and made gret care
the knyȝt bade ham leue har fare
and wepe not so sore
lle that care that we be yn
it is for our wikke syn
orthe we were wel more
and we al come no work wirch
our frend of vus schulle be
ouer londe rede we fare

div.p: Metro breve poema a rima alternata
Breve poema in italiano sull'imperatore Costantino e sua madre, Sant'Elena, la scopritrice della Vera Croce.

lg.l: con mia matre Elena Ininmeſtauocemesta uoce
tro waytroway del mio factore quellacrocequella croce
qual tene tucto el mundo Jnsuoin suo domino
